data.title: Wendy's: Biggie Traffic Problems Ahead (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
American fast food doesn't seem to have a quarter off!

Sadly for Wendy's (NASDAQ:WEN), the issues go way deeper than capital allocation—as I mentioned in my last piece.

You're probably tired of hearing me repeat that casual dining restaurants are outperforming QSRs by a long shot. And it's very simple: they deliver much better value-per-dollar.

Why would anyone shell out for a small value meal at a limited-service spot when, for just three to five bucks more, you can grab a way better, full-sized meal like Chili's (EAT) 3-For-Me, Applebee's (DIN) Really Big Meal Deal, or even Red Robin's (RRGB) new Big Yummm?

That's the question a lot of guests are asking.

And, as you can probably guess, the same thing happened again in Q3. Casual dining chains were once again the top performers.

Something happened again here.

If you take a closer look, the push on value offerings has nudged traffic back up for some QSRs, landing them in second place month after month (it was one of two segments that saw some same-store sales growth this September).

Even with some tailwinds for fast-food restaurants, including a strong presence in Florida (the best region in the United States for restaurant traffic), everything indicates that this was a very weak quarter for Wendy's.

Mixed Data for Restaurants, But Ugly for Wendy's

Starting from the top, there's still a gap of at least 100 bp between eating out and eating at home year-to-date through August (remember, we don't yet have September data due to the government shutdown).

The gap isn't as wide as it was in mid-2023, when operators still saw room to offset the drop in traffic with price increases, thus keeping same-store sales growing. You can see that as soon as the first signs of a price war appeared, there was a massive slowdown in average check growth.

Just take a look at this chart from mid-2024 (when the price war intensified):

Exactly why we saw so many operators shutting down: they simply couldn't compensate for the drop in traffic with more price increases, creating a vicious cycle.

Looking at the latest data, things played out just as I noted in my Denny's (DENN) Earnings Preview.

July and August were pretty lukewarm, and there's still some debate about whether August really marked a sequential improvement over July.

The biggest discovery we made was predicting the fall in September.

Traffic fell 1.5% year-over-year, reaching its lowest reading since March, following the much-talked-about bloody February. This caused same-store sales to grow just 1.1% year-over-year, buoyed by the growth in the average check.

Adding the implied growth of the average check per guest:

What's really interesting is that Wendy's has a strong footprint (about a quarter of its locations) in Florida, Texas, and Ohio. In theory, that should help, since Florida and Ohio did better than the national average in Q3, particularly in September.

Just throwing out some numbers here, traffic fell only 2.4% in Florida and 2.3% in Ohio in September, outperforming key markets like California, New York, and Texas.

But what about Wendy's?

Well, for starters, they pulled out the worst traffic among the five largest fast-food chains nationally (-5.1% in July, -4.5% in August, and -10.2% in September).

The same was true in Florida, with three months of declining traffic, two of which (July and September) were the worst performers among the major fast-food chains. In fact, in September, traffic fell 9.4% year-over-year for Wendy's there, clearly underperforming the market there.

In Ohio, the only standout was that traffic dipped less, falling 6.8% year-over-year in September.

Is There Anything Good at Rock Bottom?

One potential catalyst I can see is breakfast—as incredible as that may seem.

If you read my articles here, you know that breakfast is the most discretionary meal of the day, with guests cutting it out as soon as spending becomes more defensive.

The rationale is quite clear.

Besides it being the most 'skippable' meal (I personally can't live without it), it's much more practical and cheaper to prepare it at home.

Wendy's bet heavily on this meal, being one of their biggest catalysts in FY 2024, ended up being one of their biggest headwinds when guests stopped going to the drive-thrus in the morning.

While some chains like Taco Bell (YUM) realized this and even allowed franchisees to skip serving those mornings to focus on the much-talked-about 'Innovation Wheel,' Wendy's continued betting on a recovery.

Looking at the traffic graph by daypart, you can clearly see that breakfast's decline began at the end of last year and intensified in February, creating a huge gap with lunch and, especially, dinner. It turns out that in August the decline becomes a little less pronounced, with traffic falling ~3.3% year-over-year, closely resembling lunch traffic.

Could this be a resurgence for breakfast?

I tend to believe not.

Guests remain quite price-conscious, and the improvements we're seeing are coming more from softer comparisons after more than a year and a half of declining breakfast traffic.

I still think we might see management commenting on something like sequential improvement in the morning, especially given the $1 breakfast biscuit sandwich promotions.

In any case, there doesn't seem to be anything major behind the traffic uptick. Looking at the data, it's actually weaker than last quarter. We know that other $1 and up items (like drinks, something they tested last quarter) didn't perform as well.

It's not just the price, but the value embedded in it.

Is Tendys a Big Deal or Not?

McDonald's made its McCrispy Strips permanent back in May, Wendy's launched the entire Tendys platform in September, virtually every major chain is turning to fried chicken.

Have you ever stopped to think about the reason behind this?

Besides the obvious observation that a mix of chicken products would alleviate COGS, since beef is still quite expensive, there's a preference—especially among Gen Z—for meals dipped in sauces, perfect for chicken.

In fact, I wrote an article about 'Saucy,' a new KFC concept inspired by chicken strips with countless sauce combinations.

It's no wonder that chicken-centric chains like Wingstop (WING), Raising Cane's, and Chick-fil-A outperformed the market last year and are expected to maintain their leadership this year as well.

Chick-fil-A, for example, reported 3.2% year-over-year traffic growth in this terrible September (even positive traffic in California and Texas, which is no small thing right now).

Taking aim at these trends, Wendy's rolled out the Tendys platform in September. Now you can grab chicken strips in 3- or 4-piece portions, with up to six different sauces like Honey BBQ and Scorchin' Hot.

But I see some problems with this platform—mainly pricing.

The first is the lack of upsell levers. If you notice, there's no price difference per chicken strip if you order the 3-piece combo ($5.49) or the 4-piece combo ($7.29), paying $1.82 per unit in both cases.

Unlike Wendy's, each McCrispy Strips costs $1.76 per unit in the 3-piece combo and $1.50 for the 4-piece combo, making the upsell more advantageous.

The second point I noticed was the price point. Consider this: Tendys costs approximately 3.7% more than the McCrispy in the 3-piece combo, but it's much more expensive than the 4-piece combo (about 21.7%).

You can search around and you'll see many guests complaining about the price and even the recent promotions.

In my opinion, Tendys should mirror McDonald's/Chick-fil-A pricing with more upsell levers.

Maybe even more important, it could be included in the $6 Biggie Bag!

It gives them a clear value edge over other platforms, brings in price-sensitive guests, and gets people to try the chicken strips by bundling them with another popular item at a promo price.

What to Expect for Q3 FY 2025?

As you can probably imagine, I'm not bullish on Wendy's, especially in the short to medium term. It's not just their capital allocation that I dislike, but also their traffic generation strategy.

Looking at other developments, they also launched the ‘Meal of Misfortune’ promotional meal in partnership with Netflix (NFLX) and also started rolling out ‘Project Fresh.’

Basically, management said they will reduce the Build to Suit program allocation by ~$20 million to invest in other areas to increase AUV (they said marketing and technology, but without much detail).

We'll probably see more color on the earnings call.

Maybe it's a top-down change in allocation strategy, huh? Wouldn’t be bad to see.

Anyway, for Q3, Wall Street’s looking for about $0.20 in EPS — basically flat from a year ago. Revenue estimates are around $535 million, roughly 5% lower than the same period last year.

We'd be talking about a net profit of approximately $35–40 million, with a midpoint net margin of 8.8%, practically flat year-over-year.

That said, since expectations are low, I think they could deliver revenue in line or even slightly beat it and miss that EPS.

I still view Wendy's stock as 'cheap for a reason,' as you can see.

In my last article, I mentioned the reasons that would lead me to re-rate Wendy's: domestic traffic reaccelerating, QSR regaining share against casual dining, and a strategic shift in capital allocation.

None of these are on our table right now.

I will maintain my 'Hold' rating.

data.title: WEG: Powering Global Electrification With Unmatched Industrial Momentum
data.description:
Investment Thesis

WEG (OTCPK:WEGZY) continues to be one of the best-positioned industrial names worldwide. It has, over time, transformed from a regional motor maker to a diversified energy and automation platform, with clear visibility into long-duration themes of electrification, grid renewal, as well as industrial efficiency. Very few participants offer such breadth, balance, as well as quality of return.

Growth visibility appears healthy. Energy and T&D keep building on healthy spending on infrastructure, while Industrial and Automation contribute stability. Its continuing R$1.1 billion Energy Unit expansion, as well as its recent acquisition of Tupinambá Energia, reinforce WEG's presence in high-value, recurring segments such as grid solutions & e-mobility.

In spite of that transition, valuation remains cheap. It trades far below its historical as well as global peer multiples, which, to me, just doesn't add up. This, to my view, constitutes an opportunity: a business that has not just demonstrated it could generate high returns on its invested capital, but now is expanding its growth horizon into structurally affluent markets.

Company Description

With its origins in 1961 in Jaraguá do Sul, Brazil, WEG S.A. has emerged as a global leader in diversified industrial technology. This conglomerate has its footprint in Energy, Transmission & Distribution (GTD), Industrial Electro-Electronic Equipment (EEI), Commercial and Appliance Motors (MCA), and Paints and Varnishes (T&V). Its product portfolio ranges from electric motors, generators to a complete range of power as well as control systems.

Recent performance

WEG Q3 FY25 results validated the steady implementation of its broadly diversified business portfolio. Consolidated net revenue was R$10.27 billion, +4.2% YoY, and 0.6% QoQ. Industrial Electro-Electronic Equipment (EEI) was the largest contributor, with local sales rising 13.3% and export sales gains of 7.1%. North American and European demand was stable, which reflects a growing presence internationally with a system-level competence.

Energy and T&D performance were of a mixed nature. Transmission and distribution orders held up, especially in North America, but generation revenue declined as big wind and solar farms were completed during the first half of the year. Domestic activity dipped (6.8%) QoQ as utilities leveled out spending schedules. My view remains that this was a temporary break, not a structural reduction, as backlog levels are healthy.

Profitability remained resilient in spite of this mix swing. EBITDA was R$2.28 billion, 2.3% higher YoY, with a 22.2% margin that remained flat QoQ. Net income was 4.5% higher at R$1.65 billion, with the benefit of financial costs being lower, as well as tenacious tax management. ROIC was 32.4%, below last time's, but in my view still outstanding after such heavy investment.

Cash generation remained steady. Operating cash flow came in at R$4.2 billion for YTD, comfortably absorbing R$2.0 billion of capex. Most of that was spent on Energy expansion as well as automation upgrades for Brazil. Purchase of Tupinambá Energia, an electronic EV-charging platform, added a complementary angle to WEG's e-mobility as well as service business model.

The quarter demonstrated precisely what I expect from WEG: consolidated growth, predictable profitability, and disciplined investment. Industrial demand as well as export momentum more than compensated for weaker generation demand, demonstrating again just how balanced the business has come out to be.

Strategic Development

At the start of October 2025, WEG announced a R$1.1 billion expansion of its Energy Unit, with a goal to double production capacity by 2028. Of the total amount, R$0.9 billion will be invested in a new Santa Catarina factory to make large synchronous compensators, turbo generators, and high-speed motors. They are sophisticated, high-capacity products that further strengthen WEG's position in grid modernization as well as renewable penetration. R$160mn will be invested to partially expand the company's Jaraguá do Sul complex by more than 11,000 sqms.

Santa Catarina provides access to skilled labor, good infrastructure, and close proximity to WEG's core engineering center. This will keep costs under control and accelerate ramp-up once production gets going. My reading of this project is that it reflects management's typical balance of ambition vs. discipline, increasing capacity, but just where long-term demand appears certain.

YTD cumulative announcement of investment amounts to ~R$2.0 billion, spread across Brazil, the US, Turkey, Colombia, Mexico, China, Portugal, and India. This international strategy counts. It's enabling WEG to move local production closer to customers, minimize logistics expenses, as well as strengthen its regional position in the power market.

Tupinambá Energia acquisition provides a further dimension. Smaller financially, it's strategically significant. Tupinambá injects software and services into WEG's e-mobility offering, bringing a complementary hardware offering in chargers for electric vehicles. It's a transaction that gets WEG out of pure manufacturing into solutions with recurring revenue prospects.

In my view, WEG isn't growing for the sake of expansion but developing capacity where expansion truly matters, as they bring in technology where it adds relevance. This is a formula that will hold sustainability in expansion while protecting returns for the subsequent cycle.

Business Prospects

For the near term, I expect EEI will continue to be a key driver of growth, driven by robust demand both in export as well as domestic markets. The demand in industrial motors and drives, especially in North America and Europe, should remain strong enough to sustain high single-digit growth through FY26. GTD will bounce back after a softer Q3, with backlog execution as well as grid modernization work across Brazil, as well as across the U.S., propelling a recovery from FY26 onwards. Growth here will likely revert back to the high single- to low double-digit range as fresh builds into capacity.

I expect MCA will continue to be fairly subdued in the short term, as household demand and appliance demand remain soft and distributors will work to adjust their inventories accordingly. I expect this segment to grow in the low-single digits to mid-single digits through FY26, gaining gradually as replacement cycles rationalize. For T&V, I expect a low single-digit growth rate, led by infrastructure as well as industrial maintenance work, as against consumer-driven volumes.

Looking ahead to FY27-FY28, I expect firmer performance across the portfolio. EEI and GTD are likely to continue as WEG's structural growth pillars, progressing in the high single- to low double-digit range as investment in automation, grid extension, and transition to energy intensifies. MCA and T&V will increase more slowly but consistently, adding stability to the group's revenue base overall. Collectively, these segments indicate a low- to mid-teen consolidated revenue path to FY28, supported by diversification, new capacities, and positive long-cycle demand trajectories.

Margins will remain in the low 20s, with expansion spending driving marginal near-term pressure. Synergistic gains from efficiency, as well as a more value-dense product, will more than compensate for that. The energy segment leans towards value-maximized, system-level work, which will support profitability despite heavy investment. Scale-up investment as well as inflation-driven pressure will continue, but cost discipline at WEG remains intact. With incremental utilization over the next one to two years, as well as offsets from automation and energy systems, margins could reach around 23 percent at FY27–FY28.

Overall, WEG's configuration appears healthy and well-rounded. Structural expansion derives from Energy and T&D, digital traction from Automation, and predictable cash flow from Industrial Solutions. It has an internal funding model for growth, a clean balance sheet, and long-cycle demand drivers that underpin sustainable revenue expansion of about low- to mid-double-digits to FY28, with earnings and free cash flow staying in lockstep. My perspective sees FY25 as about converting backlog and controlling cost discipline, with FY26 and subsequent years about scaling established foundations. WEG keeps adding high-value, high-barrier products, synchronous compensators, turbo generators, and large induction motors, matching capabilities with long-term electrification demand rather than going for near-term volume.

Financial

Valuation

WEG trades at ~24x FY26 earnings and ~16x EV/EBITDA, a meaningful discount to its own historical-average multiples of 31x P/E and 28x EV/EBITDA. Such a discount, in my view, appears too excessive for a business that generates wider margins, superior returns, and more diversification than any earlier cycle.

Compared with its peer group, valuation discounting stands out more. Global leaders in electrical equipment, such as ETN (30x P/E, 23x EV/EBITDA), OTCPK:ABBNY (30x, 19x), and OTCPK:SBGSY (30x, 18x), carry premium multiples with similar or lower medium-term development prospects. On a return basis, the value story looks more compelling. WEG's RoTC / RoE is over 19% / 30%, vastly more than peers. Moreover, its asset-light manufacturing approach and vertical integration provide a working-capital advantage. With asset turns of 1.1x (peer average closer to 0.6x), the company's model supports high cash conversion and consistent free cash flow delivery. Yet, the company trades at a 20% to 35% discount both on an earnings and enterprise value basis. That differential, in my estimation, translates into latent re-rating possibilities.

Applying the peers' average P/E (FWD) of ~25x to WEG's FY26-FY28e EPS (BRL 1.7/2.0/2.4) equates fair value estimates at BRL 44 (USD~7.9), BRL 52 (USD~9.3), and BRL 62 (USD~11.5) per share, both translating to possible total returns (with dividends) of ~10% - 50%. On an EV/EBITDA (FWD) basis, applying the same calculation with peers means ~19x equates a yet higher fair value range of BRL 47 to 65 per share, with total upside potential reaching ~60% by FY27. Even if WEG gets half the way back to its five-year history average multiples (to ~27x P/E as well as ~22x EV/EBITDA), that alone would still justify a mid-50s price in ~12-18 months ahead.

Risk

For WEG, key risks center on execution and macro exposure. A number of capacity upgrades and new product entrants increase the risk of commissioning delays or inefficiencies, which may impact short-term margins. Customer concentration with large utilities and industrial end-users also presents a risk related to project timing. Input cost inflation and FX volatility also pose sensitivities, although WEG's high degree of vertical integration and geographical diversification provides a guard. This sector also experiences ongoing pressure from competition in the environment around automation and energy systems, although WEG's breadth of engineering and depth of offerings mitigate margin erosion risk.

Conclusion

I believe WEG's story changes from transition back to consistency. It has built a platform that combines structural growth with best-in-class industry profitability. Automation and Energy are the growth engines, and diversification offers smoothing of earnings. Tupinambá Energia deal provides yet another strategic dimension, and WEG moves closer to the e-mobility and smart energy segment. For WEG's earnings, balance sheet, and rising global exposure, a discount to prior and peers does not appear justified. Once new Energy capacity gets into a groove in FY26 and backlog turns into revenue, the story should improve. I rate it a buy. The business possesses exactly the qualities investors want: a clear profile, self-funded growth, and clean execution.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SentinelOne: Expensive Today, Cheap Tomorrow If Growth Delivers
data.description:
SentinelOne, Inc. (NYSE:S) is an American cybersecurity company that provides AI-tailored security services for computers, servers, cloud workloads, IoT devices, and other endpoints. It operates under its autonomous cybersecurity platform named Singularity XDR Platform, which is driven by AI, and can autonomously detect threats across the network.

The company operates in a high-growth and high-demand landscape, and I think the trend of the cybersecurity market growth will continue. AI adoption is also tied to AI-related cyber threats, and I believe SentinelOne's business will sustain its significant top and bottom line growth.

Based on this research, the cybersecurity market was valued at roughly $245 billion in 2024 and is anticipated to double in size and reach $500 billion by 2030. This implies a 12.9% CAGR, and supports my bullish stance for both the market and the company. The company grew its revenue by 25% over the past 12 months, which highlights a strong demand for its services. If management can execute well, S appears to be well-positioned to benefit from the market growth.

That is why I think SentinelOne is a Buy with a $23 price target over the next 12 months. This represents roughly a 33% upside possibility and broader market outperformance.

FQ2 2026 Snapshot: Double-Beat Quarter Highlighting A Strong Underlying Business

On August 28, the company reported FQ2 2026 results. This was a strong quarter for the company, underscoring a double-beat performance and double-digit top and bottom line growth. The market has rewarded the company with a roughly 7% upside over the following trading day.

The cybersecurity company posted $242 million in revenue, up by roughly 22% on a year-over-year basis, and 13k above the analysts' estimates. The company delivered $0.04 in diluted EPS, a massive increase versus $0.01 in the same quarter last year, and $0.01 above the analysts' expectations.

In their presentation, management highlighted $1 billion in annual recurring revenue, which underscores 24% growth and supports my bullish outlook for the company. The cybersecurity AI name achieved a 79% gross margin, which appears to be substantial.

SentinelOne highlighted its leadership in the AI-security segment, underlining that it has expanded its platform offerings through Prompt Security, which is a pioneering security runtime solution for visibility and control over generative artificial intelligence. Management also noted that its purple AI, which is meant for simplified cyber threat hunting and response, is growing at a triple-digit pace, which appears to be impressive, and justifies the company's premium valuation.

The company underscored that it operates in a rapidly growing market, which is estimated to represent a market value of more than $100 billion in 2025. These estimates fall in line with the research I have highlighted, underlining an anticipated massive growth for the cybersecurity landscape.

Management guided for the upcoming FQ3 2026 quarter and fiscal FY2026. It expects $256 million in the following quarter, highlighting a 22% growth on a year-over-year basis, and a continuation of the strong business demand. For FY2026, it aims for $1 billion in revenue at the midpoint, supporting a bullish narrative and in line with the quarter's 22% top-line expansion.

I think that it was a strong quarter for the company. It represents an excellent execution from management, and if it continues, I think the company could trade at a significant premium further. To that, if the AI-related company could continue growing top and bottom line at a double-digit pace, I would argue it actually appears relatively cheap, or at least justifies its valuation. I believe SentinelOne could appear to be a compelling opportunity for investors who seek to increase exposure to cybersecurity names. S appears to be well-positioned to benefit from the broader cybersecurity solution market growth.

SentinelOne Appears To Be Expensive, But Given Its Significant Top And Bottom Line Growth, It May Actually Be Cheap

The cybersecurity company currently trades at a forward P/E of 92.05, which appears to be roughly 3.6x overvalued versus the sector median of 25.10. The company also seems to be trading at nearly 3.8x premium versus the average company in the S&P500 at about a 23x earnings multiple.

The chart above indicates that this represents an average earnings multiple the company has traded over the past 6 months, underlining a possibility of multiple expansion.

The forward PEG (non-GAAP) at 0.75 implies that this cybersecurity name may actually be cheap. This highlights roughly 2.5x undervaluation versus the sector median of 1.79, justifying the premium earnings multiple. The forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales at 4.99 and 5.79, respectively, represent a 50% premium at the midpoint that partially clouds the undervalued outlook, but also suggests that if the company can continue executing well, there is room for multiple expansion.

SentinelOne appears to be a low-leverage business, and that is another argument for a bullish stance. The company has $11.54 million in total debt, and roughly $811 million in cash. This indicates that the company could pay its debt with ease, and management has financial flexibility to use debt instruments if needed to fund its growth.

The cybersecurity company has been a phenomenal top and bottom-line growth story, so far. SentinelOne grew its revenue by 25.39% over the past 12 months, which presents approximately a 3.5x advantage versus the sector median of 7.24%. The forward revenue growth rate of 24.80% suggests 3.3x outperformance versus peers of 7.46%, and supports the thesis that revenue growth is likely to continue. This top-line performance is impressive and validates a higher premium for the stock.

The EPS long-term growth rate (3-5Y CAGR) of 122.44% highlights that SentinelOne is not solely focusing on its revenue expansion. This also reflects an 8x premium versus peers of 15.20%, and states that the stock price may follow bottom-line growth in the future. This comes in line with my expectations. I think that SentinelOne stock could appreciate in price by following its EPS expansion, rather than anticipating a higher earnings multiple rewarded by the market.

SentinelOne operates at an above-market gross profit margin (TTM) of nearly 75%, implying a 52% advantage versus the sector median of about 49%. I think this is impressive, especially considering the cybersecurity name grows its revenue in double-digits. This is another argument for a bullish narrative, and it justifies premium valuation.

The market anticipates $0.19 in diluted EPS for fiscal FY2026. This would represent about 276% growth on a year-over-year basis. Wall Street believes the company may be able to report $0.33 in diluted EPS for fiscal FY2027, which would imply 73% growth from the FY2026 estimate. I believe the company appears to be well-positioned to achieve these results.

SentinelOne is an AI-tailored cybersecurity business whose services may become crucial for businesses, considering potential threats arising from AI adoption. Recent results highlight a strong demand for its services, which supports a narrative of the company being well-positioned in a high-growth market. If it can sustain its position, it should be able to benefit from a broader market growth, which would also justify both top and bottom-line expansion.

If we apply about a 70x earnings multiple, which would be roughly in line with peers, such as Palo Alto Networks at 55x and CrowdStrike Holdings at about 136x, to a $0.33 estimate for fiscal FY2027, we come in at my price target of about $23. I think this is a rational earnings multiple for the company. It would be below its typical valuation range, similar to its closest peers, yet above both the sector median and the average company in the S&P500, highlighting a high-demand, high-growth landscape and a small-cap nature, given its roughly $5.8 billion market capitalization, which tends to be valued at a premium.

Based on the analysis, if management can deliver excellent execution, followed by double-digit top and bottom-line growth, one could argue that SentinelOne may appear cheap at these levels. I believe it presents an interesting opportunity for long-term investors who may seek to diversify in a high-growth market.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to SentinelOne, which could significantly impact the stock price performance in the short term.

The broader stock market has rallied since the April lows without a significant pullback. However, SentinelOne declined by roughly 33% over the past year and has significantly underperformed the benchmark. Although the stock corrected, it still trades at a significant premium to both the sector median and the benchmark. This may be concerning, as if sentiment dramatically changes, the broader stock market could sell off, and SentinelOne may continue the downward momentum further.

SentinelOne operates in the fierce landscape. The company competes with well-known names, such as CrowdStrike Holdings, Palo Alto Networks, and Microsoft, and they may challenge the company's pricing power, technological edge, or market share. If that materializes, the company could achieve lower than anticipated top and bottom line results, which could stress the stock price further.

I think that the cybersecurity companies are trading at a significant premium because of high-growth expectations, driven by strong demand. However, if these anticipations do not materialize, sentiment may shift, and cybersecurity disruptors could be repriced at a lower premium.

On the other hand, if none of the above materializes, the company could rebound from its recent sell-off. It remains a high-quality company, a leader in an AI-tailored cybersecurity landscape, which anticipates significant growth in the following years, driven by the broader demand.

Conclusion: Downward Momentum Appears Overdone, And I Argue That SentinelOne Is A Buy

I think that SentinelOne may appear to be a compelling long-term opportunity for investors who may seek to diversify in an AI-related name, operating in a high-demand cybersecurity market. The recent sell-off appears to be overdone, and I argue that the stock may rebound from these levels.

This cybersecurity company operates at above-market margins, which indicate high-quality services. This also supports a bullish narrative. It steadily grows its revenue and is anticipated to experience massive bottom-line growth over the next few years, followed by a broader market expansion and continued demand for its services. I think SentinelOne appears to be well-positioned to benefit from the cybersecurity market growth, especially if management can execute well.

I think SentinelOne is a Buy with a $23 price target over the next 12 months. This suggests roughly a 33% upside possibility. 36 Wall Street analysts forecast a $23.39 12-month price target, implying 35% upside potential, and almost in line with my expectation. Time will tell, but SentinelOne appears to be an interesting opportunity to consider for investors seeking an AI disruptor in a high-demand market.

data.title: PNC Financial Services Group: A Resilient Performance
data.description:
Shares of PNC Financial Services Group (NYSE:PNC) hit the newswires in recent times, in between its earnings release and a larger M&A deal announced over the summer.

It has been a while since I last covered shares of PNC; in fact, it was during the regional banking turmoil in the spring of 2023 when, in particular, smaller and regional banks suffered massive deposit outflows with interest rates spiking at the time.

Ever since, PNC has seen solid returns, better than regional banks, which in general have been lagging a bit, but worse than the biggest banks out there, as PNC seems to be in the business of consolidating here in an effort to close the gap with the leading banking group.

Some Background

PNC has been a banking conglomerate, one that for years has thrived on the back of a relationship and stake in BlackRock (BLK), one of the very important value creators and disruptors in the world of finance and investing. In 2020, it sold its last stake in the business in order to focus and double down on its traditional banking activities, at the time acquiring BBVA USA.

Ahead of the 2023 (regional) banking troubles, the company reported total assets of $557 billion in 2022, making this a large bank but smaller compared to the real large US banks. Just like the entire industry, clients which were flush with money from the pandemic had to overcome real issues, with clients being strapped by high inflation and being offered the opportunity to park cash at higher yields elsewhere.

While some banks were forced to raise interest rates offered on deposits to maintain those deposits, many banks saw near term earnings pressured in 2023. However, those banks that failed to have sufficient liquidity and could not maintain deposits were often forced to sell loans (often at discounts), with some of them not surviving the shock imposed on the system.

PNC was not immune to all of this, as the company saw deposit outflows of $21 billion (in the latter part) of 2022, down to $436 billion, resorting to more expensive forms of financing, as the company offered rates at less than a percent to its depositors at the time. However, there was a lot of room to hike payout to depositors with operating profits reported at $7.5 billion at the time, leaving a lot of room to sacrifice earnings.

Stable Waters

In the end, the headwinds from the 2023 episode alleviated fast, in part as calmness was instilled in the system and banks started to offer higher deposit rates, with these made up by higher interest received on loans as well.

In fact, the company ended up with a minimal increase in average deposits in Q4 of 2023, that is compared to the same period a year before. The immediate impact of higher interest rates offered on deposits (in comparison to the gradual increase in interest rates fetched on the loan book) took a relatively modest impact on earnings. Rates offered on non-interest-earning deposits stood at 2.48% by year end-2023 as the company furthermore had about a hundred billion in non-interest-bearing deposits outstanding as well, all while trends have been very stable in 2024; that is, the level and rates paid on deposits.

Deposits stood at $425 billion by year-end 2024 (on a total asset base of $564 billion), providing a real buffer to finance a loan book of $319 billion, with the bank reporting pretty flattish results that year. Revenues of $21.6 billion for 2024 were virtually at par compared to 2023, and amidst some cost saving efforts, operating profits were posted at $8.0 billion, with net earnings around $6 billion translated into earnings just short of $14 per share.

Starting the year around the $200 mark and with earnings power reported around $14 per share, this translated into a modest 14-15 times earnings multiple.

Initially hurt by concerns about the economy in the spring amidst the tariff rout, shares have rebounded to the $200 mark over the summer (and even a bit higher), now having fallen to $186 at the moment of writing.

What Happened?

After the company passed the stress test over the summer, the board announced a ten cent hike in the dividend, now payable at $1.70 per share per quarter, with the annual payout of $6.80 per share providing a competitive 3.7% dividend yield today.

To further grow its business, the company announced essentially a bolt-on deal in September with the purchase of FirstBank Holding Company. The bank has 95 branches in Colorado and Arizona, and is the market leader in Denver, set to add some $26.8 billion in assets, thereby growing total assets by nearly 5%. With a $23 billion deposit base and $16 billion loan book, the pro forma impact on these metrics is also seen between 5-6% for all of PNC here.

The deal is paid for to an import extent with stock. A $4.1 billion transaction value is comprised of 13.9 million shares offered of PNC and a $1.2 billion cash component. This values the bank at $0.15 per every dollar in assets, for lack of a better metric, largely in line with the own valuation here (or actually marking a modest premium). This can be attributed and justified by above-average population growth in the region and generally quality assets with a relatively cheap deposit base.

Much Anticipated Results

In between the volatile economic conditions and in recent weeks concerns about some loans originated in private markets, fraud, and Jamie Dimon's cockroach comment, bank earnings were much anticipated. PNC delivered in this respect, with third quarter revenues up 9%, split nicely between interest and non-interest income, with provisions for credit losses actually down substantially on the year before.

However, the allowance for losses is a bit sharper, narrowing that cushion a bit. With expenses managed in a tight fashion and provisions being down, reported earnings of $4.35 per share were particularly strong, trending close to $17 per share here, although aided by relatively low charge-offs.

In terms of deposits, the company took in nearly $9 billion of new deposits over the quarter, with this standing at $432 billion, all looking relatively healthy. This is not including the contribution from FirstBank, of course, a transaction seen closing early in 2026. Moreover, the business is quite diversified, having substantial asset management, brokerage, capital markets & advisory, card & cash management functions as well. This, of course, is outside the core residential and commercial banking and lending activities.

And Now?

Frankly, PNC has managed to perform pretty stably in recent years, which, given this background of 2023, is a real achievement and relief. This comes amidst some real momentum seen in the business, certainly in recent quarters, and following an interesting bolt-on deal recently announced here.

Furthermore, valuations are very reasonable at a low double-digit earnings multiple, at 1.3 times book value multiple, or 1.7 times if we look at tangible book value. However, these multiples are largely in line with regional banks, as the company has a benefit in that it really is a bit too large to be classified as a regional bank. Multiples fetched by the largest banks are a bit higher, but not that much higher.

So that means that this is largely a macro play here, with the observation applying to the industry at large. So, in between this, I like the ambitions and achievements of PNC here, yet the reality is that this remains largely a macro play, although a near 4% dividend yield and growth prospects bring some appeal to investors here.

data.title: Flagstar Bank: Transformation Offers Nice Upside... If You Accept The Risk
data.description:
October 24th was a pretty good day for shareholders of Flagstar Bank, National Association (NYSE:FLG). Shares of the company rose around 5.1% in late afternoon trading after management announced, before the market opened, financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Earnings per share came in line with what analysts anticipated, while revenue exceeded forecasts.

This is great and all, but what really matters are the efforts that management is taking to reinvent the enterprise. Back in January of this year, I talked about the firm, then named Flagstar Financial after previously being known as New York Community Bancorp, and noted that it made for an interesting but speculative prospect that was not made for the faint of heart.

That company had experienced significant volume quality over the last couple of years. But management was continuing to make changes, including reducing commercial real estate exposure, growing its commercial and corporate banking activities, growing its residential mortgage business, and more. It seemed particularly interested in reducing its multifamily portfolio and its office portfolio, while simultaneously taking other measures aimed at boosting productivity. Fast forward to today, and the most recent results we have from management do show some nice progress here.

In fact, I would say that the picture is getting quite exciting. There are certainly risks, and I maintain my stance that the business is not a good opportunity for those who are more cautious. But I do think it has the potential to offer massive upside should things go right. Because of this, I don't mind having rated it as a speculative Buy at this time.

A solid quarter

The third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was pretty solid for shareholders. Net revenue for the company declined from $623 million last year to $519 million this year. However, that revenue ended up being $1.7 million above what analysts were hoping to see. Bottom line results were essentially positive. The firm went from a loss per share of $0.79 to a loss per share of $0.11. And in that regard, the company did fall short of the $0.08 loss that analysts were hoping to see. But on an adjusted basis, the firm matched the $0.07 per share loss expected of it, which marked a big improvement over the $0.69 per share the business lost on an adjusted basis in the third quarter of 2024.

This is great to see, but I do think we need to dig deeper into some of the financial performance of the business. When we look at some other metrics, I would say that the picture is more positive than negative. In the chart above, you can see certain income statement data for the most recent quarter compared to the same time last year, as well as for the first nine months of this year compared to the same time last year. What you can see is a nice jump in net interest income. Keep in mind that this is after stripping out provisions for credit losses. From the third quarter of 2024 to the third quarter of this year, the firm's provision for credit losses plunged from $242 million to $38 million.

Just by the very nature of how things work, this data was not reflected in the net revenue of the business. And we saw a similarly large increase for the first nine months of this year compared to the same time last year.

Some of this improvement can be chalked up not only to the decline in the provision for credit losses that the company endured, but also from a rise in net interest margin. In the most recent quarter, the net interest margin for the institution was 1.91%. That's up from the 1.79% reported a year earlier. However, for the first nine months of this year, the 1.82% reported by the company did fall short of the 2.01% reported last year. So clearly, this is a work in progress.

That same chart also shows non-interest income and net profits. While for the most recent quarter non-interest income declined, that was only because of a major reduction in fee income, and a $34 million net return on mortgage servicing rights that the company saw last year that is not recurring in nature. By comparison, this year, these figures were only partially offset by the $22 million in its net gain on investment securities. But the most important thing is that overall profitability for the company improved drastically. And the one thing that I find most exciting about this is the fact that compensation costs are declining, having fallen from $316 million to $242 million.

Management is truly making a concerted effort to turn the ship around. And I will also say that they are implementing other initiatives aimed at growing the business. Because I am always looking for ways to make more money, I'm not ashamed to say that I have taken advantage of one of its offerings. In fact, between whenever I signed up for it a couple of months ago and the end of this month, the company is offering up to $500 in the form of a cash bonus for anybody who opens up a new checking account, who also meets the standard of setting up one or more direct deposits to $500 or more in the first 90 days, and who maintains at least a $1,000 minimum deposit in that account. Considering it is easy for me to switch over my bank account info, I have personally taken advantage of this opportunity. And no, I am not getting paid to tell you that.

We are seeing declines in deposits still, but that comes with a caveat. From the end of last year to the third quarter of this year, the company did see its overall deposits plunged from $75.87 billion to $69.15 billion. About $6.1 billion of this deposit decline came from the firm's decision to reduce brokered deposits. But that does still leave a small drop of around $0.6 billion of organic deposit declines.

Having said that, if it means improved profitability, I'm not necessarily taking issue with it. We have seen some other changes as well. For instance, during the quarter, the company reduced multifamily loans by $1.5 billion. And commercial real estate loans in the amount of $473 million were ultimately taken off its books.

These changes have allowed the company to reduce the high cost brokered deposits that it has, while simultaneously reducing debt slightly. It has fallen from $14.43 billion at the end of last year to $13.18 billion this year. We have seen other changes in the balance sheet as well, such as a decrease in cash and cash equivalents, but an increase in securities.

Earlier this month, management completed a reorganization of its operations. This was aimed at simplifying its organizational structure by eliminating its bank holding company. On top of this, the firm is working to deploy expanded product offerings and capabilities dedicated to middle market companies, larger corporate enterprises, and in the specialized lending space. And in all likelihood, we can chalk up continued reduced exposure to commercial real estate as part of its plan.

At the same time, management is working on growing its commercial and industrial loans by originating more of those. In fact, from the second quarter of this year to the third quarter, the firm saw certain strategic areas, which would fall under specialized industries, its own financial and leasing business, the specialty finance space, and the regional commercial and corporate space, grow originations by $595 million. Simultaneous to this, over the span of just a single quarter, loan commitments under these categories jumped $748 million. And the company claims to have a pipeline of $1.8 billion of commitments as it seeks to completely reinvent itself.

Unfortunately, asset quality does still remain problematic. While the company has seen a major reduction in its net charge offs relative to average loans, as pictured above, return on assets and return on equity are both negative. In the charts below, you can see how the company stacks up against five other similar banks using these metrics:

  • Hancock Whitney Corporation (HWC)
  • Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation (AUB)
  • First Financial Bankshares, Inc. (FFIN)
  • Ameris Bancorp (ABCB)
  • United Bankshares, Inc. (UBSI).

Without exception, it is the worst of the group.

As disappointing as this is, investors buying into the firm should be doing so because they believe in the long term vision that these restructuring activities offer. For this year, management anticipates an adjusted loss per share of between $0.36 and $0.41. However, with the firm’s initiatives pushing net interest margin up materially over the next few years, we should see some nice improvements. This year, management anticipates a net interest margin of only 1.85% to 1.95%. Next year, it's expected to grow to between 2.40% and 2.60%. And the year after, it should come in at between 2.80% and 2.90%.

That would certainly put the company on the right track. And, if everything goes according to plan, these changes will boost adjusted earnings per share to between $0.75 and $0.80 next year before pushing it up even further to between $2.10 and $2.20 next year. This could have major positive ramifications on the company, especially considering how cheap shares of the business are.

In the chart below, you can see how the firm aligns against the same five banks I compared it to already using both the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In the subsequent chart, you can see how much upside the company would experience for its share price, on both an aggregate basis and on an annualized basis, if it ends up trading at either the low end of these five competitors or at the average of them, or by the end of 2027. As a note, I did remove the most expensive of these five competitors for the purpose of using the average approach when it comes to both the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. This is because it seemed like a big outlier.

We can see similar amounts of upside if we assume that management is correct about earnings per share growth. Right now, if we take the midpoint of guidance for 2027, we would get a firm that is trading at a price to earnings multiple of 5.6.

In the first chart below, you can see how these other five banks are currently valued.

And in the subsequent chart, you can see the annualized upside that the company would experience if it were to trade at either the low or the average multiples of these firms. On the low end, we would be looking at an annualized return of 30.7%. And using the average approach, it would be 53.1%.

Takeaway

To be very clear, as clear as I can be, Flagstar Bank is currently a risky prospect. Be that as it may, with this risk also comes significant upside potential. Management is certainly making progress in some key areas, and the share price movement seen on October 24th is proof that the market is recognizing these efforts. For those who can't handle the risk, the best thing to do is to stay away. But for those who don't mind some volatility and uncertainty, this prospect deserves a speculative Buy rating.

data.title: Nokia: AI Potential Unfolding Yet Still Significantly Undervalued
data.description:
Introduction

Since the last time I covered Nokia (NYSE:NOK) and called them a “Healthy And Undervalued 5G Leader Ready To Shine After Rate Cuts,” the stock is up about 25% thanks to several improvements, yet I believe there is still plenty of potential left.

Overall, my view on Nokia hasn’t changed, and their recent earnings report and macro developments such as solid results from its main competitor and rate cuts reinforce my Strong Buy rating, offering a solid long-term play on 5G, AI, defense, and other long-term trends at a very attractive price with a nice dividend plus buybacks on top.

Internal Developments

As we can see, Nokia’s first 9 months of 2025 were solid overall, with a 4% increase in sales (12% higher in Q3) but dropping margins and profits, although I generally prefer looking at the free cash flow instead, which I mention below.

Their free cash flow reached €429 million, with an improving net cash position of ~€3 billion. We still see some impact from restructuring expenses, but the results were overall solid, paying about €220 million in dividends.

Regarding the outlook, they see about €1.7 billion to €2.2 billion in comparable operating profit (vs. €1.6 billion to €2.1 billion), with a free cash flow conversion rate still between 50% and 80%. At midpoints, that’s €1.267 billion, meaning about $1.47 billion, which is solid for their $30.48 billion market cap, although their near-term outlook is much better. As a note, this increase is due to their change in the presentation of venture fund gains and losses, with Nokia deciding to scale down its passive venture fund investments following a strategic review. Besides this, their operational outlook remains unchanged.

They also mentioned how they see positive trends, especially in the demand coming from AI & Cloud customers, with an order backlog coverage of expected Q4 net sales higher than in recent years, being well on track to deliver their full year outlook.

As we can see, Nokia Infrastructure together with Mobile Networks continued to be key pillars for the company’s sales and saw some growth thanks to AI and Cloud customers, although their margins fell due to a one-time benefit in the prior year from a provision reversal. Meanwhile, Cloud and Network Services continued to grow very well, while Nokia Technologies continued to be a very solid income stream that reflects Nokia’s tens of thousands of vital patents and excellent R&D history.

Nokia also expanded well into serving enterprise clients and licensees, and this diversification away from telecom providers could be key for the company’s next stages of growth. And, also worth mentioning, not only did North America record a 13% increase, but India and the rest of APAC delivered 24% and 23% growth, respectively, while China and Latin America fell (they were not significant pillars anyway), while Europe continued to be a relatively slow and steady market for them.

Financially, based on their latest report, Nokia remains in a great position, having one of the best balance sheets in the industry. We see current assets well above the current liabilities, with a lot of cash and even a net cash position. Their PB got to about 1.35 now, although it was trading even at or below 1 not long ago, which is very good for a tech company that relies on assets that generally aren’t recorded on the balance sheet.

I’ll give again the example of their hundreds of billions invested in R&D and invaluable families of patents, and Alphabet’s (GOOG) YouTube, Cloud, Gemini, algorithms, ad platforms, etc. that simply can’t be recorded on a balance sheet unless acquired, which leads to significant gaps between equity and the real value of a business. In Alphabet’s case, we’re looking at assets valued at potentially more than a couple trillion that can’t be recorded due to the current accounting practices, which I believe to be quite outdated in this age.

In other words, traditional accounting standards fail to capture the immense economic value of internally developed intangible assets, meaning a company’s true worth can be significantly understated compared to what its balance sheet shows, especially in tech. It’s one thing to value an office building, some industrial equipment, a fleet, or a mine (although these too can vary depending on market conditions), while assets like a company’s proprietary code, intellectual property, data, algorithms, brand power, etc. are a completely different story.

They also announced several deals, such as the recent partnership with Nscale to accelerate AI data center deploys, as well as a larger deal with Vodafone (VOD) where they are one of the key providers together with their competitors.

Macro Developments

Similar to Nokia, Ericsson (ERIC) also had a solid quarter even though its revenue actually dropped by 9.1% YoY. Their stock jumped ~20% when the earnings came out, with the company expecting overall average results despite the uncertainty persisting in terms of tariffs and the broader macroeconomic environment. Probably even more important for the market was the fact that Ericsson also pointed out how AI opportunities are rapidly arising, with AI applications becoming key drivers for network investments.

This could be a sign that the recovery and pent-up demand that Nokia’s previous CEO talked about are finally unfolding, although I’d personally need more than just a quarter to confirm it, especially given the currently very unpredictable environment.

Again, gold was close to reaching $4,400, while other precious metals are also having their best year probably in history for a very good reason. This comes as the result of the market finally “waking up” after all the focus and hype around AI or other noise seen in the market for the past couple of years, while precious metals’ fundamentals got better and better without the prices following, with gold for example trading sideways since 2020.

Of course, gold prices don’t affect Nokia, but it’s more about the message they send for the market - or rather about the market’s opinion that can continue to pressure their stock. This comes especially after rate cuts, recently-renewed trade fears with China, and overall high political uncertainty, so it’s no wonder that precious metals are performing as they do.

If the market's fears that led to record levels in gold and other precious metals come out to be true, we're looking at a very bad environment where Nokia's potential clients may be inclined to invest even less, which has been a trend they've noted over the previous couple of years.

As I mentioned before, I believe we’re looking at a slow environment especially in North America due to rate hikes. Since Nokia relies especially on telecom providers, seeing them scale down on their investments in response to higher interest rates shouldn’t be a surprise, as these companies rely on massive amounts of debt. On the other hand, rate cuts should boost this investing activity significantly, as these companies would have more leeway thanks to the greater financial flexibility as their interest/refinancing expenses costs go down.

And, as a final note that’s more about an opportunity rather than an actual development, I’d like to point out how Justin Hotard used to work for Intel (INTC) until not long ago, when he took Nokia’s CEO position. Something that I believe is now more likely is seeing a state-supported joint venture focused on several potential industries, from AI to 5G or even defense now.

Nokia has been actively doing this kind of partnership even before, but I feel like Hotard might be in a bit of a better position and more familiar with the US environment than Nokia’s previous CEO - which I’ll mention again that I respect very much and admire for what he did for Nokia and the other couple of large companies he ran. For reference, Nokia had several deals such as delivering the first cellular network on the Moon with Intuitive Machines (LUNR) - working with NASA - but it’s never really been some announcement that made it “click” for the market, but that could change now. As they said during the Q3 report, we might be in for an important announcement in this regard which I’m really looking forward to:

At our Capital Markets Day in New York on November 19th, we will share our strategy to unlock the full potential of our portfolio and the steps we are taking to focus the company to deliver growth and operating leverage. The AI supercycle is accelerating demand for providers of advanced and trusted connectivity. Nokia is uniquely positioned to be a leader in this market.

This potentially closer economic relationship between the US and EU could be a thing especially if trade pressure on China and other states continue. But still, that doesn’t mean execution risks don’t exist or Nokia will now grow significantly thanks to AI or a recovery in the industry. As Ericsson also pointed out, this remains a very uncertain environment, with the recently reignited tariff threats fueling even worse fears. So, even if I believe there’s a lot of long-term potential, that doesn’t mean the near-term will be great as well.

Updated Valuation

Let’s also refresh the valuation with the latest FCF estimate and their new net debt/shares outstanding. I’ll use the updated €1.267 billion in free cash flow for 2025 (the midpoints of their new expectations), reaching €1.7 billion in 2026 (this is ~€100 million below the midpoint of their expectations). Let’s assume a 5.25% annual growth for the remaining 3 years of the first period thanks to rate cuts gradually leading to better demand especially in AI and telecom, followed by an only 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years to be extra careful, with a 2.75% terminal growth rate thanks to accelerated AI use. I’ll assume a 6.75% WACC (slightly above my previously estimated 6.5%; assumptions: ~7.35% cost of equity, ~5.1% cost of debt), with €3 billion in net cash now and 5 413 billion shares outstanding, as of the new Q3 report. This gets us to an enterprise value of €45.02 billion and an equity value of €48.02 billion, meaning a price of €8.87, or about $10.28 at current exchange rates.

Conclusions

Overall, Nokia remains a Strong Buy for me given their undervaluation, strong dividends and buybacks, excellent financial health and several industry tailwinds such as AI that offer solid long-term potential. Risks remain especially in the near-term as a result of the uncertain environment persisting, on top of their new CEO’s yet-to-be-proven ability to run the company, but I believe the overall long-term potential is well worth the risk.

data.title: Aya Gold And Silver: A Growing Pure Play Silver Miner With Leverage To Silver Prices
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Black Flag Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Aya Gold & Silver (TSX:AYA:CA) is a silver producer and developer in the middle of a massive expansion that’s increased its silver production levels triple 2024 in the coming year, promising to be one of the lowest cost silver producers in the world when operations hit maturity. Aya’s current focus is on executing their ramp up plan, with recent Q3 updates showing strong progress along various operating metrics. Silver producers have become more expensive across the board in the last few months with silver prices hitting record prices and Aya is no exception. Its valuation today is more expensive than peers and prices in fully ramped production, which the company has not quite achieved yet. Nevertheless, with extremely low production costs and expansion CapEx behind it, it presents a high risk-high reward opportunity for investors expecting silver prices to hold above $35/oz. In addition, it is in the early stages of developing a second site, Boumadine, which could provide significant increases in value should the deposit prove economically viable.

Business Overview

Aya Gold and Silver (AYA:CA) is one of the one of the only pure play silver mining companies (ironic given the name, it produces no gold today). It wholly owns and operates the Zgounder Silver Mine, its main resource, and is advancing a much earlier stage Au-Ag-Zn-Pb project (Boumadine), with both sites located in Morocco. The stock is on a tear lately, with a ~45% rise in the last 6 months, though it has underperformed silver miners as a whole.

Aya’s valuation today is primarily driven by its producing Zgounder mine. It has recently executed an expansion plan, which has massively increased silver production over 2024, and is ramping the project up to steady state. Management is doing a solid job executing their plan. With an expected all-in sustaining cost (AISC) of $9.58/oz, Zgounder has the potential to be one of the highest-margin silver mines in the world. At full production, Aya expects ~6.8 Moz of Ag annually.

2025 has Zgounder transformed from a smaller, underground operation into a much larger mine running both underground and open-pit processes. New management took over Aya in 2020 and since then has been actively growing the company. They increased M&I resources 860% by 2022 and published a feasibility study for the expansion plan the same year, with the aim of increasing production from ~2 Moz Ag/year to an expected ~7-8 Moz Ag/year by 2024.

As is often the case with new construction, things didn’t go exactly according to plan. First, silver pour was expected Q1-24 and was only achieved by end of 2024. Guidance for 2025 production is ~5 Moz Ag, last reaffirmed in Q2. Nevertheless, management has done an excellent job at operating in my view, with the project completed on budget and current operations scaling well.

The Zgounder Deposit

Zgounder is the 2nd largest silver deposit in Africa and uniquely contains native silver as its dominant mineral. This enables high recoveries and low life of mine (LOM) production costs. Zgounder is an entirely silver deposit containing an estimated ~71 Moz Ag in Proven and Probable reserves with ongoing drilling. While few would consider it a tier one jurisdiction, Morocco is arguably as stable as it gets in that region of the world.

Per the Zgounder expansion plan, operations include both underground and open pit operations. Open-pit operations are estimated to be far less expensive, with a COG of around 47 g/t vs 85 g/t for underground reserves. Feeds are expected at around ~250 g/t Ag in the feasibility study. The plan called for LOM to be 11 years, though I suspect this will be increased as drilling continues.

Aya’s previous sensitivity analysis from their feasibility study suggested an NPV of $927 million at a silver price of $36/oz. At current valuations, EV/NPV is 1.64, which is certainly on the high side, particularly for an operation still ramping into steady state (though silver prices are today well above $36/oz, using that provides some sense of what margin for safety looks like).

I have recomputed an estimate of present value from 2026 onwards (since all initial CapEx is completed) using a more conservative discount rate of 8%. I’ve made a few simplifying assumptions based on the initial cash flow schedule presented in the feasibility report, including assuming that 2025 production comes in at 5 Moz Ag and that 2026 production comes in at the ~8 Moz Ag that was initially expected in 2024 (~2 years slippage in timeline, same total LOM production). I then assume that values for sustaining CapEx, tax rates, etc are the same as those detailed in the study. This is mainly illustrative at this point to demonstrate valuation sensitivity to silver prices and we’ll hopefully have more clarity by end of the year.

In general, you have to assume pretty high silver prices to be sustained over the LOM for the valuation to make sense today based on this metric.

Recent Results

2025 (and early 2026) have been primarily focused on ramping up production. Execution has been strong this year and silver prices have helped cash flow. With the exception of feed grade, which management warned about, operational metrics have improved across the board. The mill is seeing a significant ramp up and recoveries look great (despite minimal increases in grade).

Q3 has continued this trend. Silver production hit 1.35 Moz, a 29% increase. Head grades improved slightly to 146 g/t with impressively higher recoveries and an 11% increase in ore processed. Assuming that all underground ore went to feed the mill and, given the stockpile depletion of 99.1 Kt, that leaves 89.4 Kt of ore from the pit that fed the mill with an unfortunately low implicit grade of 133.6 g/t. Aya noted that Q3 focused on stripping and waste movement operations, likely causing the significant dilution seen from the pit here. I’d expect this to improve going forward.

Underground operations are now bringing in ~1300 tpd at an average grade of 158 g/t. These values are noted to have stabilized and will feed the mine through the end of the year. Q4 should be even better, with the mill running well above nameplate capacity at 3700 tpd and recoveries >90%, coupled with 150K tonnes of stockpiled ore to supplement any feed deficits (albeit, stockpiles are low grade at 143 g/t). They also expect open-pit ore production to be above 2000 tpd by Q1-26, which should start decreasing cash costs materially in the new year. The company has noted in the past that open-pit mining costs were around half underground mining costs.

Q3 silver prices averaged $39.40/oz so revenue for the quarter should be around ~$53M. Cash costs for the quarter are difficult to predict given the higher rates of underground mining, stockpile depletion, increases in silver recovery rates, and higher head grades but a reasonable expectation is for it to land in the same ballpark as Q1 and Q2 cash costs. Assuming the midpoint of Q1 and Q2 operating cash flow margins (~21.7%), we can expect operating cash flows to come in at around ~$11.5M. That being said, this is likely a fairly conservative number and I wouldn’t be surprised to see cash flows come in the $13M-$16M range.

Forward Production

Management affirmed guidance of 5-5.3 Moz silver production in Q2. Given the Q3 data in hand, my estimates suggest that, at least the low end, of guidance is in sight and requires a ~15% increase in production over the Q3 rate. Q4 should be able to process the material necessary to hit targets and the main question will be whether or not grades will be sufficient.

The latest update didn’t provide cash costs for the quarter but guidance suggests a cash cost of $15-$17.50/oz for the year, significantly below the cash costs achieved in Q1 and Q2 of $18.93 and $21.26, respectively. To hit that, we’d need to see significantly lower cash costs in Q3 and Q4, which feels fairly unlikely unless Q3 cash costs come in at around ~$10/oz. Average grades are expected at 170-200 g/ton, though both Q1 and Q2 have been significantly below that at 163 g/t and 140 g/t processed (note average grade mined in those periods was 151g/ton and 138 g/ton), respectively. Higher recoveries will offset this a bit but, as it stands, rough math suggests they need Q4 feed grades of ~159 g/t to hit the low end of annual guidance. The current underground feed should supply that grade, with the key being dilution on the current pit operations.

Should management hit annual targets, and assuming silver prices hold up such that they can achieve an average selling price of $40, revenue would be around ~$60 million in Q4. Given ongoing operations, I suspect it’s fair to assume a similar operating cash flow (OCF) margin of around ~22%, which would yield ~$13 million in Q4. Year end totals would be around $40 million, making the current EV/OCF a rich ~37x.

2026 doesn’t look tremendously better at Q4 run rates. Cash costs are simply too high right now but, again, this is expected as the mine ramps up to steady state production.

But the plan is for much better right?

Short answer, yes. The Zgounder Expansion plan called for average annual production of 6.8 Moz at an AISC of $9.58/oz (cash costs of $7.36/oz). Current operations are operating above planned milling rates (3700 tpd vs 2700 tpd) but well below the projected grades of 257 g/t. Management telegraphed that this year would have sequencing and dilution that affected grades.

I’ll note that should cash costs be cut in half in 2026, that adds an extra ~$10/oz of margin, roughly resulting in operating cash flows of ~$107 million (and OCF margins of ~50%). At that point, even with silver prices averaging $35/oz for the year, the current EV/OCF drops to around ~14x. This calculation assumes production of ~6.2 Moz of silver.

Given its low production costs, Aya’s cash flows are very sensitive to pricing. To get a sense of what this looks like at steady state production, I’ve calculated a proxy for operating cash flow as revenue minus costs using the AISC as the cost/oz.

This simple analysis illustrates how sensitive cash flows are to changes in silver price. At high silver prices, cash flows changes start to track changes in silver but at lower ranges, significant leverage is present. Note that high silver prices make present valuations look very reasonable at projected operating costs.

Boumadine

Aya is in the early stages of developing a Au-Ag-Pb-Zn deposit, also in Morocco, of which it has an 85% stake. It’s still early and no PEA has been released, which is expected later this year. Management has done a good job of growing resources, key for any development company. It’s spent nearly $50 million on drilling over the last two years and drilling is ongoing, with ~$14 million in drilling costs during 2025.

The current mineral resource estimate points to indicated resources of:

  • 15.1 Moz silver at 91 g/t → @ $30/oz this is $453 million

  • 449 Koz gold at 2.78 g/t Au → @ $3000/oz this is ~$1.3 billion

  • 145 Kt zinc at 2.8% → @ $3000/t this is ~$435 million

  • 44 Kt of lead at 0.85% → @ $2000/t this is ~$90 million

Inferred resources are significantly higher, but those are highly speculative today. The project is currently under active drilling to advance the project towards a preliminary economic assessment (PEA). Should this deposit prove viable, it could add $100s of millions of value to Aya. However, at this stage valuing purely on in situ indicated resources, I’d value this somewhere around $50-$100 million today. Proven viability would add to this significantly but at current valuations, this doesn’t provide much of a back stop to share price anyways. A PEA is expected later this year, and positive results there could certainly make this worth hundreds of millions in the current environment. Positive results are not a given, as this is a more complex polymetallic deposit and will likely take significant ongoing capital to develop.

Risks

The primary risk today is silver price, especially at current valuations. Should prices fall I’d expect the share price to collapse along with it. Given the volatility of junior producers, and the 100% exposure to silver, I could easily see a drawdown into the $5-$8 range should silver prices stumble (if we were so lucky as to see anything in the $5 range, I'd be a heavy buyer). Operationally, I see timeline risks as the primary threat. Should management guide a longer ramp up time, I’d expect the shares to dip considerably. Management has executed well thus far, so I view this risk as low (relatively, all new operations experience hiccups and growing pains). Lastly, there’s always the impending risk of only operating a single deposit. Any incident that shuts down or reduces production has no buffer. Further investment into Boumadine will also draw down on cash flows long term.

Outlook

Aya Gold and Silver boils down to a high leverage play on silver with optional upside from its Boumadine deposit. Its valuation today looks rich almost any way you look at it. You’re essentially paying for de-risked, scaled production today. Should production jump to the originally predicted values in the coming years of around 7.5 million ounces of silver, then at a $30/oz silver price Aya could be looking at around ~$130 million in after-tax cash flow, giving a much more reasonable EV/CF of ~11.75. At that point, exposure to rising silver prices and optionality on Boumadine could make this a compelling opportunity even at current valuations. Ramp up timing through the rest of the year and early next year will dictate how reasonable this is and serve as catalysts for the price, along with release of the Boumadine PEA.

Silver mining companies have shot up in recent months, making valuations high across the board. The ETF Global X SIlver Miners ETF (SIL), which tracks a broad group of silver miners, is up >60% in the last few months. It’s hard to find direct comps for Aya because most silver producers also mix in other metals but for some helpful context on EV/OCF(TTM) valuations for peers:

  • Pan American Silver Corp. (PAAS): ~17x
  • First Majestic Silver Corp. (AG): ~23x
  • Hecla Mining Company (HL): ~26x
  • Endeavour Silver Corp. (EXK): ~101x
  • Aya Gold and Silver (forecasted for year): ~37x

That being said, silver prices are trending higher. Industrial demand for silver is on the rise long term with solar demand now accounting for >30% of demand, to say nothing of its value as a safe haven asset. Mine supply is difficult to increase owing to broad mining trends of rising costs, decreasing grades and long permitting times. Silver production is still largely a byproduct of mining other metals (>70% comes from a byproduct), thus its production is tied to the influence of mining other metals. 2025 will mark the 5th consecutive year of silver supply deficits.

At current prices, even with management’s solid execution thus far, it seems like a difficult buy without an admittedly very high risk tolerance. Then again, everything is starting to feel that way to me right now and I do believe we're in the midst of a long term revaluation of metal pricing across the board. Downside risk is high in my opinion but I could easily see upside as high as 2x from here should cash costs stabilize and silver prices stay high. If you want a silver miner for your portfolio, and can tolerate volatility through 2026 should ramp up timelines slip, Aya is a very good option. Personally, I added to my (small) position on the 15% drop in price this week.

Conclusion

Aya Gold and Silver is a high quality silver miner, providing investors with a growth opportunity and high leverage to silver. While no junior is without problems, it has an excellent management team that has largely delivered or exceeded on promises. Their wholly owned Zgounder deposit provides the opportunity for industry leading low cost silver production. While the valuation today appears to be factoring in fully scaled production, which is not a given yet, all signs point towards higher production and lower costs in the coming quarters. Should silver prices remain elevated, and miners remain in demand, I could easily see the price here running another 2x. Boumadine provides another catalyst to the upside, should positive results be achieved. However, current valuations leave little downside buffer and buyers should be aware that any drops in silver price or stumbles in execution will likely cause severe drawdowns. Ultimately, Aya is a solid choice for risk tolerant investors looking for pure silver exposure.
