data.title: Garrett Is Still A Bargain Even After A Blowout Report
data.description:
Introduction

Garrett Motion (NASDAQ:GTX) delivered another strong quarter on 10/23/25, bolstering my thesis that the company will benefit from the bridge between today’s ICE and diesel vehicles to tomorrow's zero-emission vehicles. At the same time, the company continues to make progress in advancing its alternative technology. The most important part of my thesis is that ICE, diesel, and non-electric commercial vehicle turbo sales are a viable path to robust investor returns for the next five to 10 years and may still be underappreciated by investors, even after a more than 20% run-up following Q3 earnings.

In my last article on GTX, I outlined my bridge thesis and called the stock my best contrarian idea, as I believed the gasoline, diesel, and industrial turbo sales story was underappreciated by the market. Q3 earnings from both General Motors (GM) and GTX appear to be further validation of my thesis. In that last article, I spent more time on the zero-emission technology story for GTX, in addition to the bridge thesis. In this article, I will focus mainly on the gasoline and data center opportunity for GTX and highlight the most important parts of the Q3 earnings.

Headline Numbers From Q3 2025

GTX delivered sales of $902 million, growing by 9.2% on a reported basis and 6% in constant currency. Gross profit increased by 12.0% YoY, and the company delivered great operating leverage, with Income before taxes accelerating by 34.2%. Given the massive $84 million buyback executed during the quarter (share count declined by 7.4% YoY), diluted EPS grew by 58.3% to $0.38 per share. GTX continues to expand both adjusted EBITDA margin and adjusted FCF margins. The adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 18.2%, up 75 bps YoY and 235 bps on a 2-year stack. Adjusted FCF margin expanded to 11.9%, up 327 bps YoY and up 593 bps on a 2-year stack (*author calculated stack and exact bps provided).

This is the operating leverage I have anticipated GTX achieving on top of moderate sales growth, and now that the company is seeing sales improve, it's time to see if the thesis is validated and if the stock has more room to run in the near term and if the long-term outlook is as strong as it appeared to me in the past.

Beyond the buybacks, GTX’s board raised the dividend by 33%, to $0.08 per share, and the company prepaid $50m on its term loan. Capital returns to shareholders remain a priority, with 75% of adjusted free cash flow targeted for shareholder returns.

While 9% sales growth might not seem like high growth, I call this a blowout quarter because the company had been written off as a casualty of the BEV growth story. With that narrative likely dying, in my estimation, let's move on to the longer-term thesis.

My Bridge Thesis Confirmed - Now Expectations Might Be Changing

Investment Thesis

Part of my thesis for GTX is what I call the Bridge Thesis. ICE and Hybrid vehicles are the bridge to a fully BEV future, and GTX is a key cog in the supply chain during this transition. Sales volume through the earnings cycle should remain healthy during the transition, and GTX’s investments in zero-emission technology could carry the company from there.

My thesis is that BEV adoption growth is slowing and will continue to normalize following the rapid early-adopter growth seen from 2019 to 2024. PHEV market share is actually growing faster than BEV share since 2023, and a plug-in hybrid may be far more practical for anyone who rents, lives in an apartment instead of their own house, and does not have easy access to public chargers at their workplace.

The Trump administration has pulled back on funding of low- and zero-emission investments, including EV charging funds, and there were signals that growth might have been slowing. In early 2025, Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk made significant cuts to its Supercharger team, and even though the network buildout continues, you don’t make cuts to your infrastructure buildout if you expect growth to accelerate in the near term. Deliveries and Production figures for TSLA, the leading BEV seller and best proxy for BEV demand growth, began to see declining growth rates and eventually declining demand.

The General Motors (GM) Q3 2025 earnings report pretty much confirmed my thesis, providing a very nice read-through for GTX. CFO Paul Jacobsen had this to say about EV demand:

In October, we are not surprised to see EV demand soften significantly, and we expect this trend to continue into early 2026 before we see what the natural demand is for EVs.

CEO Mary Barra had this to say:

Now with an evolving regulatory framework and the end of the federal consumer incentives, it's clear that near-term EV adoption will be much lower than planned. This is resulting in higher variable costs as we expect to utilize less capacity across our EV plants and supply chain.

All of this drove our decision to transition Orion Assembly from EV to ICE production and to sell our joint venture-owned cell plant in Michigan to LG Energy Solution.

These statements epitomize GM’s rationale for shifting more heavily towards increasing ICE vehicle production, and the efficiency gains that turbochargers provide are already embedded into the technology designs and performance capabilities that consumers have come to expect from ICE vehicles.

Well, if GM and others are going to produce and sell more ICE vehicles, someone will have to supply them with more turbochargers, right?

I had already sold off my GM position in late 2024, so other investors got to enjoy that ride. While I missed out on GM’s post-report gains, I doubled my position in GTX, expecting the business momentum and shifting winds in the industry to lift the stock.

Now that GTX has delivered back-to-back positive quarters, and the industry headwinds might be subsiding to some extent, we might see analysts' estimates improve in the days and quarters ahead. This provides the potential for sustained share price increases.

Forward Estimates Should Get Revised Upward

GTX raised its FY 2025 guidance by narrowing its sales range from $3.4 billion to $3.6 billion to a range of $3.5 billion to $3.6 billion. The midpoints of net income and adjusted EBITDA were boosted by 9.6% and 1.6%, respectively.

But the industry outlook and operating leverage that GTX benefits from could spark the biggest revisions from analysts.

Analysts estimates, which likely have not been revised yet, are calling for a 3.0% CAGR in sales from now through 2027; operating income growth of a 5.0% CAGR and EPS growth of a 13.9% CAGR are expected.

With improving business momentum shifting tailwinds from the slowing transition to BEVs, I expect that GTX’s gasoline turbo sales, stable commercial sales, and emerging data center genset solution will support growth for the foreseeable future, while its investments in zero-emission solutions provide optimism that the business can last in the long term.

The Turbos For Gensets Opportunity

GTX’s mature, flexible manufacturing is powering a nascent business supplying its industrial turbochargers for data-center generator sets. Its mature, high-volume turbo manufacturing and discipline should allow it to scale reliably where newer, unproven entrants to data center backup power suppliers may struggle. Management quantified “over $100 million” of 2025 sales from stationary-power applications (much of it is data-center related) and disclosed that it captured over $40 million in awards for components for new genset projects in Q3. Management said gensets-related sales grew double digits YoY. I do not view GTX as a pure data center play, but I think gensets can become a meaningful and sticky stream of industrial revenue that requires less R&D and capex to develop than entirely new products. I wouldn’t expect the ceiling on data center genset revenue to be any more than 10% of GTX revenue in the future, but it’s one more growth lever for the company.

Valuation

GTX earns a B- valuation score on Seeking Alpha and a quant rating of 4.77, or a strong buy.

The stock trades at a trailing EV/EBITDA multiple of 7.16x and a forward multiple of 6.70x. Both are significantly below the sector median.

With the company's operating leverage kicking in, the stock just needs a sentiment shift and market acceptance that the growth story hasn’t died. It has already seen some of that shift in 2025, but if the stock can earn a re-rating closer to the sector median, it has the potential for a 30-40% increase or greater.

Risks

GTX is in a cyclical and consumer-dependent industry. If the economy suffers, GTX could see end-market demand fall. This risk could impact the stock’s potential for a re-rating and the company’s revenue growth prospects.

If the EV market share pullback is only a temporary phenomenon, the sentiment could once again turn negative, and gains from here could rely on the company’s zero-emission technology development.

Conclusion

GTX’s Q3 report and the read-through from GM strengthen my confidence in my bridge thesis and that the company is positioned to win, both in the bridge years of ICE and hybrid demand and in the long-term shift toward zero-emission technologies. Q3 validated what I believed earlier this year: the market has underestimated the durability and cash-generating potential of the turbo business, even as GTX quietly builds future optionality in electrification and industrial applications. With improving guidance, strong operating leverage, accelerating capital returns, and tailwinds from resilient demand in ICE, hybrids, commercial vehicles, and now data-center gensets, the near-term setup remains attractive.

Despite GTX’s run-up this year, the stock remains a good value, and I reiterate a buy but downgrade the stock from a strong buy.

data.title: American Financial Group Baby Bonds Fly Under The Radar
data.description:
Our article today is about an established name in the insurance sector that continues to attract our attention with its stability, financial results, and dividend policy. In the following lines, we will focus on the current state of American Financial Group, Inc. (NYSE:AFG) and its publicly traded securities.

Company basics

  • Total assets Q2 2025: around $31 billion
  • Current market capitalization: around $11 billion
  • Credit ratings:

  • 10-year total return comparison with S&P 500 (SP500):

  • Baby bonds

AFG has four baby bonds (NYSE:AFGC), (NYSE:AFGB), (NYSE:AFGE), and (NYSE:AFGD):

All bonds trade below par, have high duration, and are rated by the major credit agencies as "investment grade." Moody's rates them with a Baa2 credit rating, and S&P rates them with a BBB credit rating.

The call dates of all four have passed, so they are callable anytime. All have a yield to worst of around 7%.

AFGC is the bond with the highest yield to maturity - 6.88%. It has 34 years to maturity, with a maturity date on 12/15/2059. On 11/22/2019, 8 million debentures of AFGC were issued, each with a par value of $25, or gross proceeds of 200 million. AFGC pays interest distributions of 5.125% per annum, or $1.28 annual amount, in the form of $0.3203125 quarterly. Below are the basic metrics of the bond:

We are facing a lot of questions about yield calculations, and we calculate them using the XIRR function in Google Sheets, which is supposed to be the most precise way when dealing with ex-dividend dates. It is a bit more work than just finding a random bond yield calculator, but at least we try to ensure the data is accurate. Below you can see AFGC's yields to maturity at different prices:

Comparison with peers

Below are shown the baby bonds in the insurance sector, with high duration and credit ratings by the major credit agencies. We see our baby bonds are among the highest yields, especially AFGC, with its yield to worst close to 7%.

  • OTC bonds

The OTC bonds of AFG are rated Baa1 by Moody's, or a notch higher than the baby bonds of the company, but their yield range is between 4 and 5.6%. We preferred a one-notch lower credit rating but a much higher yield.

We have chosen OTC bonds with high duration that have ratings between BAA1 and BAA3 by Moody's, and there are close to 100 bonds that trade with yield to worst lower than 6%:

1% yield spread with such a high duration gives us more than 10% capital appreciation potential if, at some point, the market decides to treat AFG's exchange-traded debt similarly to the overall bond market.

Conclusion

AFG's baby bonds trade below par, have an investment-grade rating, and offer yields of around 6–7%, with AFGC standing out as the highest yielding. Compared to the peers in the sector, AFGC shows better spreads, better yield, and a more balanced credit profile. This makes it an attractive choice for investors looking for stable fixed income with moderate risk in the insurance sector.
